cityCenter.name: Central Geneva
cityCenter.description: Geneva harbour and Old Town around Pont du Mont-Blanc and Place du Molard

routes.name: Léman Express / CFF

price.note: Free with the complimentary 80-min TPG/CFF ticket from the machine in baggage claim

routes.tips: Pick up a free Geneva Transport Unireso ticket from the red machine just before baggage claim — valid 80 minutes on all TPG buses, trams, trains, and Mouettes within the city. Hotel guests also get the free 'Geneva Transport Card' for the whole stay.
pros: Connects to all Léman Express lines
cons: Cornavin is 1km from the Old Town — short walk or onward tram
Crowded at rush hours and with skis

routes.name: TPG Bus 5

price.note: Free with airport Unireso voucher / Geneva Transport Card

routes.tips: Useful if your hotel is in Pâquis, Place des Nations, or near the UN — bus 5 runs the full lakefront diagonal across the city.
pros: Direct to Pâquis and UN quarter

routes.name: TPG Bus 10

price.note: Free with airport Unireso voucher / Geneva Transport Card

routes.tips: Best for travellers heading to Old Town or Eaux-Vives — bus 10 crosses the Rhône via Pont du Mont-Blanc and stops at Rive.
cons: Traffic on Pont du Mont-Blanc at rush hour

routes.name: Taxi

price.note: Metered. Typical city centre fare CHF 35-50; supplements for luggage and night service

routes.tips: Official ranks at the Arrivals level. Taxis run on a meter — Geneva taxis are among the more expensive in Europe. Larger MPV taxis available for ski groups.
pros: Useful with skis or large luggage
cons: Heavy traffic on Route de Meyrin at rush hour

routes.name: Uber

price.note: Variable; Uber Black available for premium pickups

routes.tips: Pickup zone is on Level -1 (Arrivals) — follow rideshare signs from baggage claim. Bolt does not operate in Geneva; Uber is the main app option.
cons: Surge pricing during events and ski-day weekends

routes.name: Ski Shuttle (AlpyBus, Mountain Drop-Offs)
routes.frequency: On demand or scheduled in ski season

price.note: Per person to Chamonix; private transfers higher

routes.destination: Chamonix, Verbier, Les Portes du Soleil, Megève
routes.tips: Most travellers heading to French Alpine resorts use a dedicated shuttle rather than entering Geneva itself. Book in advance for Saturday transfers.
pros: No need to enter Geneva city
cons: Saturday traffic on the Mont Blanc tunnel route

trainStations.name: Genève-Cornavin
trainStations.description: Geneva's main rail hub — Swiss intercity (IC/IR) and TGV Lyria to Paris, plus all Léman Express suburban lines and the SBB connection to the airport.

trainStations.name: Genève-Eaux-Vives
trainStations.description: Cross-border Léman Express hub — fast service to Annemasse, Annecy, Évian, and connections deeper into Haute-Savoie.

generalTips: Pick up the FREE Unireso transit ticket from the red machine in baggage claim — valid 80 minutes on TPG buses, trams, Mouettes water buses, and CFF/Léman Express trains within Geneva. This alone makes a taxi or Uber from the airport unnecessary for most travellers.
All hotels and registered accommodations issue a complimentary Geneva Transport Card on check-in, covering local public transport for the entire stay.
The airport-to-city train takes only 7 minutes and runs every 10-15 minutes — the fastest airport-to-centre link in Europe.
Bring your passport: the Léman Express crosses into France at multiple points, and many day trips (Chamonix, Annecy, Lavaux is Swiss but check) involve a French-side journey.
Geneva taxis are expensive (CHF 35-50 from the airport, CHF 50+ within the city). Public transport is excellent and free for tourists — use it.
For ski trips to French resorts, dedicated airport-to-resort shuttles are cheaper and easier than entering Geneva.
Avoid renting a car for a city-only visit — parking is expensive and the centre is largely pedestrianised. A car only makes sense for Alpine excursions.
